title: The Return of the King
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-31-23 -AM- The Return of the King - 1 Thessalonians 4_13-5_11 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Finishing the Advent Series with the Second Advent
subpoints: The Advent season is ultimately not just about Christ having come, but about His coming again
Because He came, He will come again

outline.title: What This Passage Clearly Teaches
subpoints: As surely as Jesus came 2000 years ago, He will return (1 Thess. 4:16)
Believers will be raised from the dead (1 Thess. 4:15, 17)
Unbelievers will be judged when Jesus returns (1 Thess. 5:3)
Belief in His return shapes how we live right now (1 Thess. 5:8)
These doctrines are meant to encourage — not produce conflict (1 Thess. 4:18; 5:11)

outline.title: What This Passage Does Not Teach
subpoints: No mention of the resurrection of unbelievers in this passage
No secret rapture: "thus we shall always be with the Lord" describes the final state, not a temporary catching up
The word for "meet" describes going out to escort a dignitary back into the city — we return with Christ immediately

outline.title: The Resurrection Hope for Believers
subpoints: Some believers had died in Thessalonica, causing confusion about the resurrection
Paul's answer: the Lord will bring back to life those who died as believers (1 Thess. 4:14)
At the return of Christ, the dead in Christ will rise first, then the living will be transformed (1 Thess. 4:16-17)
We were made to exist body and soul — the resurrection is the eternal state, not just souls in heaven

outline.title: The Day of the Lord: Judgment for Unbelievers
subpoints: The day of the Lord is a day of judgment (1 Thess. 5:2-3)
The emphasis is not only on the world "out there" but on hypocrites within the church
Judgment will come suddenly, like a thief in the night, like labor pains upon a woman
For unbelievers, it will be like labor pains ending in a stillborn — sudden, inescapable, and without comfort

outline.title: Prepared and Not Surprised: The Believer's Response
subpoints: The return of Christ will not catch the true believer by surprise
Believers are children of light and children of the day (1 Thess. 5:4-5)
The church will be prepared because it behaves according to its identity
Sobriety of body and mind while waiting: faith, love, and the hope of salvation as breastplate and helmet
God has appointed us not to wrath but to obtain salvation (1 Thess. 5:9)

summary: In this final Advent sermon preached on New Year's Eve, Pastor Lyro argues that the Advent season is ultimately about Christ's coming again — not merely a celebration of His first coming. Drawing from 1 Thessalonians 4:13-5:11, he identifies what the passage clearly teaches: the certainty of Christ's return, the bodily resurrection of believers, the judgment of unbelievers, and the call to live rightly in light of all these truths. He also clarifies what the passage does not teach: it says nothing about the resurrection of unbelievers, and the term often used to support a "secret rapture" actually describes going out to meet a dignitary and escort him back — meaning believers will meet Christ and immediately return with Him. The sermon then addresses two distinct audiences. For believers who have lost loved ones, Paul's answer is profound comfort: the dead in Christ will rise first, and then the living will be transformed — because we were made for existence body and soul, not just as disembodied souls in heaven. For the hypocrite in the church, the sermon is a solemn warning: the Day of the Lord comes unexpectedly, like a thief in the night, and its judgment is inescapable. But for those who truly belong to Christ, that day will not overtake them as a thief. They are children of the light — they know Christ is coming, they are expecting Him, and they live now with sobriety, faith, love, and hope. The sermon closes with the encouragement that God has appointed believers not to wrath but to salvation, and whether dead or alive at Christ's return, they will be with Him forever.
transcript:
## Introduction: Finishing the Advent Series with the Second Advent

We are coming to the end of the 2023 Advent season. As we finish considering the coming of Christ for this season, I want us to finish this series by looking at the fact that Christ is coming again. That's really what the Advent season is about. It's not so much a celebrating the fact that Christ came, but that because He came, He's going to come again. So let us take time today to look at the second Advent of Christ — and especially the fact that because of His grace appearing to us, there are things that we need to be doing now.

We are going to read from God's Word out of 1 Thessalonians chapter 4, starting at verse 13 and reading through chapter 5, verse 11:

"But I do not want you to be ignorant, brethren, concerning those who have fallen asleep, lest you sorrow as others who have no hope. For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so God will bring with Him those who sleep in Jesus. For this we say to you by the word of the Lord, that we who are alive and remain until the coming of the Lord will by no means precede those who are asleep. For the Lord Himself will descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of an archangel, and with the trumpet of God. And the dead in Christ will rise first. Then we who are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air. And thus we shall always be with the Lord. Therefore comfort one another with these words.

"But concerning the times and the seasons, brethren, you have no need that I should write to you. For you yourselves know perfectly that the day of the Lord so comes as a thief in the night. For when they say, 'Peace and safety!' then sudden destruction comes upon them, as labor pains upon a pregnant woman. And they shall not escape. But you, brethren, are not in darkness, so that this Day should overtake you as a thief. You are all sons of light and sons of the day. We are not of the night nor of darkness. Therefore let us not sleep, as others do, but let us watch and be sober. For those who sleep, sleep at night, and those who get drunk are drunk at night. But let us who are of the day be sober, putting on the breastplate of faith and love, and as a helmet the hope of salvation. For God did not appoint us to wrath, but to obtain salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ, who died for us, that whether we wake or sleep, we should live together with Him. Therefore comfort each other and edify one another, just as you also are doing."

## What This Passage Clearly Teaches

The first thing I want us to see is what this passage clearly teaches. As sure as Jesus Christ came 2,000 years ago, He will return for us. We see that clearly in chapter 4 verse 16.

This passage also clearly teaches that we will be raised from the dead. That's taught in verses 15 and 17 of chapter 4 — no dispute about that. If you believe the Bible, you must believe in the bodily resurrection.

The passage also clearly teaches that unbelievers will be judged when Jesus returns — we see that in verse 3 of chapter 5. What for us will be a time of blessing will for unbelievers be a time of judgment.

This passage also clearly teaches that because Christ will return in the future, we live rightly now — chapter 5 verse 8. The fact that we believe He's returning causes us to live now for His glory in a certain way.

And this passage clearly teaches that these doctrines are most encouraging to those who believe in Jesus Christ. Look at chapter 4 verse 18 and chapter 5 verse 11 — Paul says he's teaching these things so that we can encourage one another with them. The study of the last things tends to create a lot of conflict in the church. Yet Paul says the purpose of these teachings concerning the return of Christ is that we go around encouraging each other to obedience and to the glory of God.

## What This Passage Does Not Teach

I want to also note some things that are not mentioned in this passage that people sometimes import into it.

First, you will not find any mention of the resurrection of the unbeliever in this passage. Paul speaks of believers who are alive at the return of Christ, believers who are dead at the return of Christ, and unbelievers who are alive at the return of Christ — but says nothing about unbelievers who are dead at the return of Christ. We find that elsewhere in Scripture, but not here.

Second, this passage does not teach a secret rapture. Look at verse 17: "Then we who are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air. And thus we shall always be with the Lord." If this teaches some sort of secret rapture where you're caught up with the Lord in the clouds, then note that verse 17 says "in this manner we shall always be with the Lord." If you hold to this as a secret rapture, you have to be content with eternity being just that — floating people with the Lord in the sky — because that's what the verse tells you in that manner.

But there's more to it. The word translated "meet" here is often used to describe what citizens would do when a dignitary came to visit their town. The mayor and the most important people of the town would leave the city limits, meet the dignitary at the edge of town, and then walk him back into town. That's what Paul is saying: when the Lord returns, we are going to meet Him and come back here immediately — not after some other sequence of events. We meet Him and return with Him, and forever we are with Him in the state we are at that time.

## The Resurrection Hope for Believers

Some believers had died in Thessalonica after Paul had visited them, which created confusion among those who remained. What's going to happen to them? We thought Jesus Christ was going to come back in our lifetime. But He hasn't, and people have died.

Paul's answer: the Lord will bring back to life those who died as believers. Verse 14: "If we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so God will bring with Him those who sleep in Jesus." And at the return of Jesus Christ, the Lord will bring back to life those who died believing in Him. Those who are alive will also join the Lord — but after the ones who died in Him. Verse 15: "We who are alive and remain until the coming of the Lord will by no means precede those who are asleep."

So when the Lord comes back, the believers in Christ — those who are alive — will also be changed, but that will not happen before the graves give up the bodies and the souls that are united in perfection to those who have gone before us. On that day the Lord will call them from the dead. As Paul says in 1 Corinthians 15: "Behold, I tell you a mystery: we shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed — in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, and the dead will be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed."

These are physical bodies at the coming of the Lord Jesus Christ — able to enjoy and worship God in Christ physically and eternally, because you and I were made to exist body and soul. Where the dead in Christ are now, in heaven, is not the eternal state. The most desirable thing is body and soul together — seeing Christ face to face and worshiping Him. That's what we're destined for.

And this is extremely good news, especially when faced with the death of a loved one. When a loved one dies in Christ, we sorrow, we cry, we grieve — please do. But we sorrow with hope. They are going to come back to life and worship the Lord forever.

## The Day of the Lord: Judgment for Unbelievers

But Paul says more. Paul calls the return of Christ "the Day of the Lord" — verse 2 of chapter 5: "For you yourselves know perfectly that the day of the Lord so comes as a thief in the night."

As Paul speaks of that day, the Scripture tells us it is a day of judgment. Paul borrows from Amos 5:18 and 20 where the prophet says: "Woe to you who desire the Day of the Lord! For what good is the Day of the Lord to you? It will be darkness, and not light." And this is the day of judgment upon unbelievers.

But I want you to notice that the emphasis of the Old Testament and the emphasis of Paul here is not on the unbeliever out there. It is on the unbeliever right here in the church of Jesus Christ. We tend to think all these judgment passages are about the big, bad world out there. But God is more concerned with the hypocrite in the midst of His people. God is more concerned with you who are here today faking a profession of faith, pretending to be a Christian. That's what this judgment is coming for.

This judgment that is brought upon the unbeliever at the return of Christ will come upon him when he least expects it. Look at chapter 5 verse 3: "When they say, 'Peace and safety!' then sudden destruction comes upon them as labor pains upon a pregnant woman. And they shall not escape."

Paul uses the analogy of labor pains. Labor comes unexpectedly and painfully — but usually, what helps a woman endure labor pains is knowing that a child will be at the end. This is not the description here. It's like labor pains that end in a stillborn child — nothing to show for it, and no end in sight.

## Prepared and Not Surprised: The Believer's Response

But the main point of this passage is encouragement for believers. Look at chapter 5 verses 4 through 7: "But you, brethren, are not in darkness, so that this Day should overtake you as a thief. You are all sons of light and sons of the day. We are not of the night nor of darkness."

The return of Christ will not catch the true believer by surprise. Believers know Christ is coming back — and they are like a family expecting a guest: they don't know the exact hour, but they know the guest is on his way and they are prepared. That's the picture of the parable of the ten virgins: they had oil in their lamps. That's the picture of the servants with girded waists and burning lamps — waiting for the master to return from the wedding.

Any view of eschatology — any view of the end times — that does not teach that our life now counts, and that we should live in righteousness according to the Word of God, is a problematic view. If your view of the end times does not cause you to live rightly right now, there is a problem. Our Lord Himself teaches that the only way to be prepared for His return is to be living right now according to His word.

So how do we live? Verse 8: "Let us who are of the day be sober, putting on the breastplate of faith and love, and as a helmet the hope of salvation." We are not to be slothful, and we are not to be drunk. We wait for Jesus by being righteous and productive instead of sleeping our lives away. We are to be sober — not just don't be drunk with substances (though Paul doesn't mean anything less than that). We are to have sobriety of heart and sobriety of mind as we wait for the return of the Lord Jesus. This is Paul's well-known triad of the Christian life: faith — both our personal trust in Christ and the content of what we believe; love of God and for God that preserves us; and the hope of salvation.

And brothers and sisters, we will persevere to the end because God is working in us. Verses 9-10: "For God did not appoint us to wrath, but to obtain salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ, who died for us, that whether we wake or sleep, we should live together with Him."

Brothers and sisters, our Lord is coming back. And because you believe in Jesus Christ, whether you are dead or alive it won't matter — He will bring you back to life, will unite your soul to your body seamlessly, and forever you shall be with Him. You are going to see Christ as He is, and you shall be like Him.

So we encourage, we comfort, we build each other up — because Christ is coming again. It will be well with our souls because He is coming again. We look to His appearing with great hope, with faith and love, because Christ is coming again and He's coming for us. Rejoice in that.

Father in heaven, thank you for Your word. We thank you that Your word is clear. Father, we pray that Your Spirit will plant the things taught here true to our hearts, that we might grow in Christ. Help us to look for His coming, that we might live soberly and righteously — that we would not be given to drunkenness in whatever form, and to slothfulness, but that we would be busy working for the King. We ask in His name. Amen.
